case_name: Jahmal Tillman and Jirus Tillman Individually and O/B/O Rose Tillman v. Lammico, Practice Protection Trust Fund, the State of Louisiana, Through the Board of Supervisors of the Louisiana State University and Agricultural Mechanical College, and Lsu Health Sciences Center, West Jefferson Holdings, LLC, Woldenberg Village, Inc., Durga Sure, M.D., Frank Culicchia, M.D., Joshua Lowentritt, M.D., and Michael Puente, M.D.

summary.brief: Jahmal Tillman and Jirus Tillman Individually and O/B/O Rose Tillman v. Lammico, Practice Protection Trust Fund, the State of Louisiana, Through the Board of Supervisors of the Louisiana State University and Agricultural Mechanical College, and Lsu Health Sciences Center, West Jefferson Holdings, LLC, Woldenberg Village, Inc., Durga Sure, M.D., Frank Culicchia, M.D., Joshua Lowentritt, M.D., and Michael Puente, M.D.. Medical malpractice case involving cardiology.

evidence_index.text: On March 25, 2012, Ms. Tillman, presented to the emergency department at West Jefferson Medical Center via EMS complaining of a severe headache and left-sided weakness.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: A CT scan of the head . . . revealed a right basal ganglia bleed extending into the right lateral ventricular posterior horn.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Sure, a neurosurgeon, recommended that Ms. Tillman be admitted to the Intensive Care Unit and receive a repeat CT scan in six hours.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dilantin [(phenytoin)] was started for seizure prophylaxis.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Ms. Tillman was . . . admitted to the Cardiac Care Unit under the care of Dr. Frank Culicchia with a diagnosis of hemorrhagic stroke.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Freiberg evaluated Ms. Tillman at Dr. Culicchia’s request and noted that Ms. Tillman was doing well, with no clear need for continued prophylactic, anti-convulsant medication.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Sure noted that Ms. Tillman’s neurological examination was improved, but ordered that Ms. Tillman was to continue receiving Dilantin.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Ms. Tillman’s neurological examination was described as ‘stable,’ at which time transfer to rehabilitation was approved by neurosurgery.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Ms. Tillman continued to receive Dilantin, and she complained of symptoms indicative of an allergic reaction, including itching.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Ms. Tillman was transferred to Woldenberg on April 27, 2012, on which date Dr. Lowentritt assumed care for Ms. Tillman and ordered that Ms. Tillman would continue to receive phenytoin.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Dalinkus noted that he highly suspected [Drug Reaction/Rash with Eosinophilia and Systemic Symptoms (“DRESS”)] syndrome present upon admission due to phenytoin.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Ms. Tillman ultimately passed away on May 22, 2012.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Zwerner opined that if DRESS would have been considered while Ms. Tillman was at Woldenberg, as he believes it should have been, the phenytoin stopped, and supportive therapy promptly started, then there is an excellent chance that her reaction to the medication would not have had the severe progression that it did.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Zwerner

evidence_index.text: We find that the trial court abused its discretion in granting Respondents’ Motion to Exclude and in limiting Dr. Zwerner’s testimony.
evidence_index.speaker: Court conclusion

evidence_index.text: Accordingly, we grant Relators’ writ application and reverse the trial court’s June 17, 2024 judgment.
evidence_index.speaker: Court conclusion

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Patient presented to the emergency department via EMS with severe headache and left-sided weakness.
initial_differential: Other causes of headache and weakness
critical_findings: Right basal ganglia bleed extending into the right lateral ventricular posterior horn

requestables.name: CT scan of the head
requestables.description: CT scan to evaluate for intracranial hemorrhage

reveal.result_summary: Right basal ganglia bleed extending into the right lateral ventricular posterior horn
reveal.decision_impact: Prompted admission to ICU and repeat CT scan

requestables.name: Neurosurgery consult
requestables.description: Consultation with neurosurgeon for management of intracranial hemorrhage

reveal.result_summary: Recommended admission to ICU and repeat CT scan in six hours
reveal.decision_impact: Led to ICU admission and monitoring

timeline_phases.name: Emergency Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents to ED with acute symptoms

timeline_phases.name: Initial Workup and Management
timeline_phases.description: Patient admitted to ICU and managed for intracranial hemorrhage

options.description: Discontinue Dilantin as per neurologist's suggestion
options.clinical_reasoning: No clear need for continued prophylactic, anti-convulsant medication
Continued despite suggestion to consider discontinuation

expected_action_court.description: Discontinue Dilantin as per neurologist's suggestion
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert opinion suggested no need for continued medication

explanation.what_should_have_happened: Discontinuation of Dilantin should have been considered

patient_outcome.description: Patient developed DRESS syndrome and passed away

key_findings: Failure to discontinue phenytoin despite signs of allergic reaction
Causation established - DRESS syndrome led to patient's death

malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Discontinuation could have prevented DRESS syndrome

tests_performed.result: Right basal ganglia bleed extending into the right lateral ventricular posterior horn

court_findings.legal_significance: Failure to act on signs of allergic reaction
